risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Randomisation to one of two study arms is done by sealed envelope method using the permuted block design, with blocks of two and four subjects to maintain the adequacy of randomisation. A computer program generated a random number sequence, which was used to allocate subjects to the treatment or wait-list control group." Comment: This explicitly reports a computer-generated random number sequence and permuted block randomisation, both indicating a truly random allocation sequence.

Quote: "Randomisation to one of two study arms is done by sealed envelope method ... The randomisation schedule and sealed envelopes were prepared by an individual who worked closely with the Centre for Statistical Design and Analysis and was not directly involved in the study." Comment: The paper explicitly reports a sealed-envelope allocation method and that the schedule/envelopes were prepared by an independent person not directly involved in the study, which supports prevention of foreknowledge of the next assignment. Although the description does not explicitly say "opaque" or "sequentially numbered," the reported mechanism is clearly intended to conceal allocation from recruiters before enrolment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Blinding is not possible for the HARMONY Study due to the wait-list control design of the study." Comment: Participants necessarily knew whether they were receiving immediate MBSR or were on the wait-list, and personnel delivering the intervention also had an unblinded wait-list design overall. Because the main outcomes were blood pressure measures and related behaviors that could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of assignment (e.g., lifestyle changes, expectations, adherence), lack of blinding creates a plausible performance bias risk.

Quote: "Blinding is not possible for the HARMONY Study due to the wait-list control design of the study. However, those delivering the MBSR therapy are not informed as to which arm subjects are randomised to." Comment: Outcome assessment for the primary outcome was ambulatory blood pressure (ABP), recorded by an automated ambulatory blood pressure monitor with mean values calculated from device readings. This is an objective measurement and is unlikely to be influenced by lack of blinding of the assessor, even though blinding of study personnel/participants was not possible.

Quote: "The primary outcome will be examined using an intent-to-treat analysis. An intent-to-treat population is defined as all subjects in the study who completed at least one session of MBSR." Comment: The study states an ITT approach and does not report substantial or clearly imbalanced missing outcome data for the 12-week primary outcome. The review characteristics indicate 50 randomized to intervention and 51 to comparison, with outcome data available at follow-up and only 3 control participants starting antihypertensive medication (censored thereafter), which does not suggest attrition-related bias in the primary comparison.
